setup_simulation_howto.user: How do I set up a simulation on the platform?
setup_simulation_howto.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation about running simulations.## Overview — what you can do
The Open Brain Platform supports configuring and running simulation workflows from the Virtual Lab. You can:
- Build simulations interactively using the conversational Neuroagent (natural language).
- Configure small microcircuit simulations and paired-neuron simulations via the chat-driven state manager.
- Incrementally fill in settings (circuit, neuron sets, stimuli, duration, recordings) and validate before execution.
- Monitor and pay for runs using the Virtual Lab credit system.

(I checked the platform docs on simulation configuration and state management to prepare these steps.)

## Quick step-by-step: set up a simulation

1. Choose a Virtual Lab and Project
- Open the Virtual Lab for your work (labs manage credits, roles and projects).
- Make sure the project has sufficient credits to run simulations.

2. Decide simulation type and circuit
- Supported through the chat agent today:
  - Small microcircuit simulations (circuit-based, many neurons)
  - Paired-neuron simulations
- Identify the circuit or brain region you want to simulate (atlas-based circuit or a saved circuit entity).

3. Configure the simulation (recommended via Neuroagent)
- Tell the assistant what you want in plain language, e.g.:
  - “Configure a small microcircuit simulation for somatosensory cortex S1 (1000 ms).”
  - “Set up a paired-neuron simulation for two connected L5 PCs, duration 500 ms, record soma voltages.”
- The agent will:
  - Read the current configuration (so it won’t overwrite what’s already set)
  - Edit the shared configuration state (neuron sets, stimuli, duration, recordings)
  - Provide a navigation link to the configuration page if you’re elsewhere

4. Fill required fields (explicit examples)
- Circuit (by ID or name) or CircuitFromID
- Simulation length (ms)
- Node set / neuron selection (AllNeurons, ExcitatoryNeurons, InhibitoryNeurons, IDNeuronSet, or PredefinedNeuronSet)
- Recordings (e.g., SomaVoltageRecording; specify neuron_set and dt)
- Any stimuli or synaptic manipulations (Poisson input, current clamps, connect/disconnect synapses)

5. Validate the configuration
- Ask the agent: “Is this configuration complete?” or “Validate the configuration.”
- The agent uses the platform validator — it will report missing required fields or confirm readiness.

6. Estimate credits and run
- Check the estimated credit cost shown in the UI for the chosen settings.
- If sufficient credit is available, submit the run from the UI (the agent can help prepare the configuration but execution is done through the platform’s run workflow).
- After execution you’ll get SimulationResult and SimulationExecution records (SONATA outputs).

7. Inspect results
- View generated SimulationResult artifacts (traces, spike files, sonata outputs).
- Use platform tools to analyze or visualize traces and population outputs.

## Tips and best practices
- Build incrementally: start with the circuit and duration, then add neuron selections and recordings.
- Be explicit about numeric fields (duration in ms, seed values) to avoid ambiguity.
- When filtering neurons by properties (layer, mtype), use the property values available in the circuit node population (the agent can show available property names and values).
- Validate before running to catch missing fields and reduce wasted credits.
- If you’re not sure what fields are required, ask: “What do I need to set up for a simulation?” — the agent will list required fields.

## What Neuroagent/state management does for you
- Reads your current configuration.
- Applies targeted edits to the shared simulation state (no wholesale replacements).
- Can validate the state and present validation errors.
- Provides navigation links to the UI page where your configuration is visible.

## If you want, I can:
- Walk you through creating a specific config now (tell me: circuit/region, simulation length, neuron selection, recordings you want).
- Update the shared simulation state for you and validate it.

Which simulation would you like to set up now? (e.g., “Small microcircuit for S1, 1000 ms, record soma voltages from 50 L2/3 pyramidal cells”.)

submit_jobs_digital_brain.user: How can I submit jobs and get responses from the Digital Brain? What language or syntax can I use to communicate with it?
submit_jobs_digital_brain.expected_output: you submit simulation jobs through the Workflows > Simulate interface (graphical UI) or programmatically via Jupyter notebooks. The platform also has an AI chat assistant that accepts natural language queries for data exploration and literature search.
submit_jobs_digital_brain.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for how to submit jobs to the Digital Brain and the supported communication/syntax.## Short answer — use natural language
You submit jobs to the Digital Brain through the Open Brain Platform using natural language (via the Neuroagent conversational assistant) or the platform UI. You do **not** need a special programming language or query syntax for normal job submission — just describe what you want in plain English (for example: “Set up a 1000 ms microcircuit simulation of S1 layer 2/3 with 50 neurons,” or “Run a paired-neuron simulation between cell A and cell B”).

---

## How it works (step‑by‑step)

### 1) Workspace and credits
- Create or select a **Virtual Lab** (your collaborative workspace).
- Allocate credits to the project or workflow you’ll use — running simulations consumes credits.  
(See Virtual Lab documentation for managing labs, roles and credits.)

### 2) Describe the job to Neuroagent (natural language)
- Ask Neuroagent in plain English to set up or run a job. Example requests:
  - “Configure a small microcircuit simulation in hippocampus CA1 for 500 ms.”
  - “Set up a paired-neuron simulation and use default synapse parameters.”
- Neuroagent interprets the request, chooses the right internal tools, and prepares the configuration.

### 3) State-driven configuration (conversational)
- Neuroagent uses state-management to read, edit, and validate your simulation configuration:
  - It reads the current configuration (what’s already set).
  - It applies your requested changes (fills fields, adds populations, durations, etc.).
  - It can validate the configuration and tell you what’s missing or whether the config is ready to run.
- You can update incrementally (add details later), ask “What is configured?” or ask it to validate.

### 4) Review and run
- Neuroagent provides the resulting configuration and a link to the relevant UI page.
- Confirm and run the simulation (the UI will show estimated credit cost).
- Execution creates simulation entities (SimulationCampaign / Simulation / SimulationResult) tracked by the platform.

### 5) Retrieve results and responses
- After execution, results (SONATA outputs, traces, metrics) are available through the platform and can be accessed via the UI or asked about via Neuroagent.
- Neuroagent can help fetch and summarize outputs, or point you to the simulation result entries.

---

## Supported submission modes and current capabilities
- Natural-language conversation via **Neuroagent** (recommended for most users).
- State-management via chat: Neuroagent can directly modify UI configuration fields (small microcircuit and paired-neuron simulations are supported now).
- The platform tracks the PROV-style entities for simulations (SimulationCampaign → Simulation → SimulationResult) so runs and outputs are recorded and retrievable.

Supported simulation types currently documented:
- *Small microcircuit simulations*
- *Paired neuron simulations*

(Neuroagent will inform you when additional simulation types become available.)

---

## Tips and examples of phrasing
- Be specific when possible: “Set duration to 500 ms”, “Use layer 5 pyramidal cells, 100 neurons”.
- Build incrementally: start high-level then refine: “Create circuit for S1” → “Make it 1000 ms” → “Add 50 neurons from L2/3”.
- Ask for validation: “Is this configuration complete?” or “Can I run this now?”

---

## Important operational notes
- You need sufficient Virtual Lab credits before execution — Neuroagent and the UI show cost estimates.
- Neuroagent will provide links to the exact configuration or results pages in the platform UI.
- If you prefer programmatic or advanced workflows (APIs, notebooks), the platform provides tools and notebooks — ask me what specific programmatic interface or notebook you want and I’ll look up the exact docs for that (I can fetch the relevant product documentation).

---

If you'd like, I can:
- Walk you through creating and funding a Virtual Lab for your first run.
- Help draft a specific simulation request (give me the region, cell types, duration, and numbers).
- Look up programmatic/API options or example notebooks for automated submission. Which would you like next?

synaptic_input_meaning.user: What does the "synaptic input" on the current page mean? Is it a signal input from a specified position?
synaptic_input_meaning.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for how \synaptic input\ is defined on the site.## Short answer
**\Synaptic input\** on the page means a configured synaptic activation (a synaptic event or train of events) that the simulator delivers to the model. For single-neuron *synaptome* simulations you place synapses at specific locations on the morphology and specify their activation parameters (e.g., frequency). In circuit simulations synaptic inputs can also be spike-train stimuli that target neuron sets (populations) rather than a single anatomical point.

## Details from the documentation
- **Single-neuron synaptome simulations**
  - *Synaptic locations*: you explicitly define where synapses are placed on the neuron's morphology (for example a dendritic section and offset).
  - *Frequency inputs*: you set the activation frequency (how often those synapses generate events).
  - *Synaptic properties*: you can configure per-synapse parameters (mechanism, weights, release properties).
  - These options appear under the \Synaptic Inputs (Synaptome Simulations Only)\ configuration step.

- **Circuit / small-circuit simulations**
  - Synaptic mechanisms are included by default (e.g., ProbAMPANMDA_EMS, ProbGABAAB_EMS).
  - Stimuli can be:
    - *Current-clamp* to a section (e.g., soma),
    - *Spike-based stimuli* (Poisson, synchronous, sinusoidal Poisson),
    - *Targeted stimuli* that apply spike trains to specified neuron sets (not necessarily an individual morphological coordinate).
  - Synaptic manipulations and connection overrides can change properties at the connection level.

## What it is — and what it is not
- It *is* a source of synaptic events (single events or spike trains) that are delivered to the model and cause postsynaptic currents.
- It *is often tied to a location* on the morphology in single-neuron synaptome setups (so yes — you can specify a position/section + offset).
- In network/circuit contexts, synaptic input can instead be specified as spike trains targeting neuron sets or edge populations (so not limited to a single anatomical coordinate).

## Practical tips
- If you want an input at a precise anatomical spot, use the synaptome / synaptic locations step and choose the section and offset (e.g., **(section: apic[0], offset: 0.3)**).
- If you want network-style inputs (many presynaptic cells or stochastic spike trains), use spike-based stimuli targeted to neuron sets or configure the circuit’s edge populations.
- Check the \Recording\ panel to add recording locations so you can verify the local effect of synaptic inputs.

If you tell me which page or simulation type you're looking at (single neuron synaptome vs circuit), I can give step‑by‑step instructions for placing a synapse at a specific coordinate or for configuring a spike-train stimulus.

brain_malformation_simulation.user: I want to simulate a brain malformation. How would I do that? My objective is to simulate a Chiari malformation for surgical planning. How do I incorporate that to simulate a 3D brain on this platform?
brain_malformation_simulation.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation about simulations and modeling (how to create or modify 3D brain models and run simulations). I'll search the docs for \simulation".I'll check the docs for feature support (future features) about 3D/biomechanical brain or surgical simulations.## Summary — short answer
You can prototype a Chiari malformation workflow on the Open Brain Platform, but the platform currently focuses on atlas-driven brain data, cell/circuit models, and neural simulations (microcircuits, paired neurons, single-neuron). Patient‑specific biomechanical surgical planning (full 3D deformable brain, FE soft‑tissue mechanics, surgical tool interaction) is not documented as a built-in product in the docs I checked. Below I outline a recommended workflow using the platform features that do exist (Virtual Lab, data import, simulations), note where external processing is required, and give practical next steps (including who to contact if you need feature support or a custom integration).

---

## Background from platform docs (what's available)
- Virtual Labs: workspace, projects, and credits for running workflows (manage credits, members, projects).  
  Source: Virtual Lab documentation.
- Neuroagent / state management: the assistant can help configure simulation state for supported simulation types (small microcircuit simulations, paired-neuron simulations). It also supports incremental configuration and validation for those simulation types.  
  Source: Neuroagent and state-management docs.
- Platform data model and entity types include support for entities such as brain_atlas, brain_atlas_region, external_url, scientific_artifact, and simulation/simulation_campaign entities (these can be used to store or reference custom data).  
  (This appears in the platform entity lists referenced by the documentation.)

Note: I searched the product docs and future-features docs for “surgical” planning and found no documented product that directly provides patient-specific 3D biomechanical surgical simulation tools.

---

## Practical workflow to simulate a Chiari malformation (recommended approach)

High-level idea: prepare a patient‑specific 3D anatomy and biomechanical model outside the platform (segmentation, mesh, material properties, FE model or simplified geometry), import the prepared model and necessary metadata into an OBI project, then use OBI simulation capabilities where relevant (for neural/functional/atlas-based analyses) or export the model to specialized surgical planning software. The platform can host and organize inputs, run neural simulations supported by OBI (microcircuit / neuron-level), and store simulation campaigns/results.

Step-by-step:

1. Project setup
   - Create a Virtual Lab project for the case (use Virtual Lab features to allocate credits and set members).
   - Add collaborators (surgeons, imaging scientists, modelers) to the project.

2. Acquire and segment imaging (external)
   - Obtain MRI / CT for the patient (you must do this in clinical imaging software).
   - Segment relevant anatomy: posterior fossa, cerebellar tonsils, brainstem, craniovertebral junction, CSF spaces. For Chiari, the key is the cerebellar tonsil descent, foramen magnum anatomy, and posterior fossa volume.

3. Build geometry and biomechanical model (external / specialized tools)
   - Generate 3D surface meshes (STL/OBJ) and/or volumetric meshes (tetrahedral mesh) from segmentations.
   - Assign material properties and boundary conditions if you need biomechanics (finite-element) to estimate tissue deformation under e.g., CSF pressure changes, surgical bone removal, or decompression.
   - If you do not need full biomechanics, you can create a geometric modification of a standard atlas (e.g., move tonsils downward) and use that geometric model for visualization and metric calculations.

   Tools commonly used externally: ITK-SNAP / 3D Slicer for segmentation, Meshlab / Gmsh for meshing, FEBio / Abaqus / ANSYS / SOFA for FE biomechanics. (Platform docs don’t prescribe external tool choices; these are typical steps.)

4. Import assets into the platform
   - Upload derived meshes / models and metadata as platform entities. Use the entity types the platform supports:
     - external_url for references or hosted files
     - scientific_artifact for processed datasets/artifacts
     - brain_atlas_region or brain_atlas (if you create or modify an atlas region to represent the malformation)
   - Document provenance: original imaging, segmentation protocol, mesh generation, material parameters, and any validation steps.

   Note: The documentation shows these entity types exist and are used to link resources in simulation workflows. Use them to keep the model discoverable and linked to simulations.

5. Decide what you want to simulate on-platform
   - If your aim is neural / circuit-level effects (e.g., how cerebellar tonsil compression might affect local circuits, blood flow–neural coupling, or evoked responses): use platform simulation stacks (microcircuit, single-cell, paired-neuron) and reference the region(s) of interest. Neuroagent/state tools can help configure these supported simulation types.
   - If your aim is surgical biomechanics (bone resection, CSF dynamics, brain deformation): the platform docs do not show a built-in FE biomechanical surgical tool. For full surgical planning you will likely:
     - Run FE biomechanical simulations in external software, and then import results into the platform for visualization, storage, and integration with other neural models.
     - Or request a custom integration / professional support from the Open Brain Platform team (see contact steps below).

6. Run simulations and store results
   - For supported simulation types (microcircuits, paired neurons): use Neuroagent/state-management to configure and generate simulation campaigns and then execute them. The platform tracks SimulationCampaign, Simulation, SimulationResult, SimulationGeneration, and SimulationExecution entities.
   - For biomechanics executed externally: store results as simulation_result or scientific_artifact entities and link them to the project/campaign for review and visualization inside the Virtual Lab.

7. Visualization and surgical planning
   - Use the platform’s visualization features where available (3D model viewers, cell/circuit visualizers) to overlay the malformation model with atlases and circuit data.
   - Prepare reports (screenshots, 3D views, simulation outputs) to share with clinicians; include clear provenance and limitations.

---

## Checklist: inputs you should prepare before using the platform
- MRI/CT DICOM series for the patient
- Segmented masks of posterior fossa structures (tonsils, brainstem, skull base)
- Surface/volume meshes (STL/VTK/tet mesh) and a clear description of coordinate frames and units
- If using FE biomechanics: mesh + material properties + boundary condition specifications + simulation run(s) exported outcomes
- Metadata document describing acquisition and processing steps (provenance)

---

## When the platform is sufficient vs when you need external tools
- Use the platform for:
  - Organizing project assets, running supported neural simulations (microcircuit, paired-neuron, single-neuron), storing and visualizing results, and collaboration in Virtual Lab.
  - Linking patient-specific atlas regions or uploaded models as entities (brain_atlas_region, scientific_artifact, external_url).
- Use external specialized software for:
  - High-fidelity patient-specific biomechanics (large-deformation soft tissue FE, surgical tool interaction, CSF hydrodynamics) — I did not find documentation that this is currently a built-in product.
  - Advanced surgical simulation GUIs and real-time planning tools.

---

## Options if you need platform support for surgical biomechanics
- Contact platform support / product team to:
  - Ask about custom integrations, upcoming features, or enterprise services for surgical planning.
  - Request guidance on best practices for importing external FE results for visualization and linkage to neural simulation data.
- If you want, I can:
  - Help draft a concise support request summarizing your needs (files you have, desired simulation type, required outputs).
  - Or: assist you step-by-step to prepare data for import (naming conventions, metadata fields, export formats) so you can upload to the platform.

---

## Recommended immediate next steps
1. Decide your primary simulation goal:
   - Surgical biomechanics (deformation & tool planning) → prepare FE mesh & plan to run externally; import results to OBI for organization/visualization; contact support for integration options.
   - Neural/circuit effects (local networks affected by compression) → prepare atlas mapping of affected regions and we can configure supported circuit simulations on-platform.
2. If you want to proceed on-platform now: tell me which of the two goals above you prefer, and whether you already have segmentations/meshes to upload. I can then:
   - Guide you on how to format and upload artifacts, and/or
   - Start configuring a simulation campaign (for supported simulation types) using the state-management tools.

---

If you want, I can draft the support request for your surgical‑planning requirements (including the need for patient‑specific FE biomechanics and visualization integration), or I can help prepare your segmentations/meshes for import. Which would you like me to do next?

multi_neuron_simulation.user: How can I run a multi-neuron simulation on the platform?
multi_neuron_simulation.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for simulation guidance.## Overview — what the platform supports
- The platform supports multi‑neuron simulations at several scales. Currently available simulation types for multi‑neuron work include:
  - **Small microcircuit simulations** (3–20 neurons; atlas‑based or custom microcircuits)
  - **Microcircuit / region‑scale simulations** (larger circuits available via other workflows as supported)
- You can design, validate and run simulations from a Virtual Lab and Projects workspace. Running simulations consumes **credits** from the Virtual Lab.

## Quick summary (one‑line)
Use Neuroagent (the conversational assistant) or the simulation UI to design a circuit (pick brain region / neuron populations / duration / inputs), validate the configuration, then generate and execute the simulation — monitor progress and retrieve SimulationResults when finished.

## Step‑by‑step guide

### 1) Prepare your Virtual Lab & credits
- Ensure you have an active **Virtual Lab** and that it has sufficient **credits** for the planned run.
- Check or request more credits if needed (Owners/Admins can allocate credits to projects).

### 2) Choose the simulation scale and circuit
- Decide the appropriate scale (e.g., **small microcircuit** for tens of neurons).
- Select or upload the circuit entity you want to simulate (atlas‑based circuit, prebuilt microcircuit, or a custom circuit).

### 3) Configure the simulation (two options)
Option A — Use Neuroagent (recommended for quick setup)
- Ask Neuroagent in plain language, for example:
  - \Configure a small microcircuit simulation for mouse S1 layer 2/3 with 50 neurons, 1000 ms duration."
- Neuroagent will:
  - Read the current configuration (if any),
  - Apply edits to the simulation configuration state,
  - Let you refine parameters (populations, synapse models, inputs, duration).
- When ready, ask: \Is this configuration complete?\ — Neuroagent can run state validation.

Option B — Use the simulation configuration UI
- Open the simulation configuration page inside your Project.
- Fill fields: Circuit/entity, simulation duration, time step, neuron populations and counts, background/extrinsic inputs, recording probes, and any special model parameters.
- Save the configuration.

Notes:
- You can build incrementally — set high‑level parameters first and add details later.
- Supported multi‑neuron config types: small microcircuit and paired neuron are directly supported by the state management tooling.

### 4) Validate the configuration
- Validate the config before generating/executing. Validation ensures required fields are present and consistent.
- If using Neuroagent, ask it to validate; it will report missing fields and can fix them if you instruct it.

### 5) Generate the simulation campaign
- After validation, create (generate) the SimulationCampaign / Simulations. This expands parameter sweeps into concrete Simulation records.
- The platform will create one Simulation per parameter combination as part of the generation phase.

### 6) Execute the simulation(s)
- Submit the generated Simulation(s) for **execution** from the UI.
- Execution will consume credits proportional to model complexity, runtime duration, and compute resources required.
- Monitor the SimulationExecution progress status in the Project (logs, progress bars where available).

### 7) Retrieve and analyze results
- Each finished run produces one or more **SimulationResult** entities (SONATA or other platform formats).
- Download results or open visualization tools/notebooks:
  - Inspect spike trains, membrane potentials, connectivity stats.
  - Use platform notebooks (Virtual Lab) to run post‑processing analyses or visualizations.

## Practical tips / best practices
- Start small: run shorter durations and fewer neurons to check model correctness and resource usage before scaling up.
- Use test runs to estimate credit consumption.
- Record only needed variables (e.g., spikes only vs. full voltage traces) to reduce output size and cost.
- Use Neuroagent to iteratively configure and validate — it reduces form filling and helps catch missing parameters.
- Keep reproducible notes: include circuit entity IDs, SimulationCampaign ID, parameter settings, and the SimulationExecution ID for tracking.

## Commands & phrases that work well with Neuroagent
- \Set up a small microcircuit for mouse S1 layer 2/3 with 20 neurons and 500 ms duration."
- \Change the simulation time step to 0.025 ms and enable spikes and Vm recordings for population A."
- \Validate the current simulation configuration."
- \Generate the simulation campaign and run it."

## Where to find documentation on the platform
- See the Virtual Lab and Neuroagent documentation pages in the product docs for details on credits, state management, and supported simulation types. The platform docs describe:
  - Virtual Lab organization and credit management
  - Neuroagent state management (configure/validate via chat)
  - Which simulation types are currently supported (paired neurons and small microcircuit workflows)

----

If you’d like, I can:
- Configure a short test microcircuit for you now (tell me species, brain region/layer, neuron counts, and duration), or
- Walk through estimated credits for a planned run if you give the model complexity and intended duration. Which would you prefer?

aging_vs_tbi_hippocampus.ai_response:
## Summary / next step
I can design and (if you want) prepare simulation configurations on the platform that compare an *aging mouse hippocampal* circuit to an *adult mouse hippocampal* circuit after a traumatic brain injury (TBI). Before I create configs, I need a few choices from you — or I can use a recommended default design. Which do you prefer?

- Option A — I’ll propose defaults and prepare ready-to-validate simulation configs for both conditions (recommended if you want me to do the work now).  
- Option B — I’ll give a detailed experimental plan and list of parameter changes so you can implement it yourself (recommended if you want to review before I change state).

Tell me which option (A or B) and then answer the short clarifying questions below so I configure the simulation exactly as you want.

---

## Clarifying questions (needed to proceed)
Please answer any that matter; I’ll assume sensible defaults for anything you skip.

1. Scale of the model
   - Do you want a full hippocampal-region model (CA1+CA3+DG, egion\ scale) or a smaller microcircuit (e.g., CA1 microcircuit of pyramidal cells + interneurons; \small\ or \microcircuit\ scale)?
   - Default I propose: CA1 microcircuit (small/microcircuit) — tractable and captures key circuit dynamics for aging and TBI comparisons.

2. Species / ages
   - Confirm: *Mus musculus* (mouse)?  
   - Define ages: “adult” = 3–6 months; “aging” = 18–24 months (I will use 24 months unless you specify otherwise).

3. TBI model type and timing
   - What TBI phenotype should be modeled? (e.g., focal contusion with local neuron loss, diffuse axonal injury, or concussive TBI that alters synaptic efficacy/inflammation)
   - Default I propose: focal hippocampal contusion producing localized cell loss + synaptic weakening and increased background noise.

4. Key biological changes to model (I can use defaults)
   - For aging: typical choices are reduced excitatory synapse density, reduced inhibitory interneuron numbers/function, modest changes in intrinsic excitability, altered ion-channel expression, increased background noise.
   - For TBI: focal neuron loss (percent), reduced local connectivity, increased extracellular calcium/magnesium dysregulation, elevated excitability or network noise, and altered short-term synaptic plasticity.
   - Default manipulations I propose (see detailed list below).

5. Simulation details
   - Desired simulation length per trial (default 1000 ms).  
   - Number of repetitions / seeds (default: random_seed=1; can sweep seeds).  
   - Stimulus type: spontaneous activity only, or include evoked input (e.g., Poisson input / stimulus pulse)? Default: include both baseline spontaneous input and an evoked stimulus epoch.

6. Outputs / metrics to compare
   - Which outcomes matter most: spike rates, population synchrony, LFP proxy, excitatory/inhibitory balance, connection/synapse counts, evoked response amplitude/reliability, plasticity metrics?
   - Default: collect soma voltages (for spike detection), spike counts, per-population firing rates, pairwise synchrony, and mean synaptic currents.

7. Do you want me to create the simulation configuration in the shared state now (I will then validate it), or just provide the plan?
   - If “create”, I will prepare two simulation blocks (aging and TBI) and set up neuron_sets, synaptic_manipulations, recordings, and initialize parameters.

---

## Recommended default experimental design (I can implement this if you pick Option A)

High-level
- Species: **Mus musculus**
- Region: **Hippocampus — CA1 microcircuit** (pyramidal cells + key interneuron classes)
- Two experimental conditions:
  - **Control Adult** (adult mouse, baseline)
  - **Aging** (24 months): parameter changes to reflect aging
  - **Adult + TBI** (adult mouse with focal hippocampal contusion): parameter changes to reflect TBI

Core simulation settings (defaults I will use unless you change them)
- **simulation_length:** 1000 ms
- **v_init:** -80 mV
- **random_seed:** 1 (we can sweep seeds)
- **extracellular_calcium_concentration:** 1.1 mM (can be modified for TBI)
- Recordings:
  - Soma voltages for neuron_sets (All neurons, and optionally subgroups)
  - Spike times (derived from soma voltages)
  - Aggregate measures (population firing rate, synaptic currents)
- Stimuli:
  - Background Poisson input to excitatory and inhibitory populations
  - One evoked stimulus (Poisson burst or current clamp) at t = 200 ms to probe evoked responses

Specific manipulations to represent aging vs TBI
- Aging (recommended parameter changes)
  - **Excitatory synapse count reduced by 20–30%** (model by scaling connectivity or synapse counts)
  - **Reduction in GABAergic interneuron efficacy**: decrease inhibitory synaptic weights by 10–20% (or reduce number of inhibitory neurons by ~10%)
  - **Increase spontaneous background noise** (Poisson rate +10–20%)
  - **Alter short-term plasticity**: increase depression (fewer available vesicles / increase failure probability) — optional
  - **Slight changes in intrinsic excitability**: small reduction in HCN / leak currents (if e-model parameters are available) — optional

- Adult + TBI (recommended parameter changes)
  - **Focal cell loss**: remove 10–30% of neurons within a local hippocampal subvolume (use DisconnectSynapticManipulation + IDNeuronSet to remove)
  - **Reduced synaptic connectivity locally**: reduce connection probability / mean synapse count by 20–50% in the damaged subregion
  - **Increased excitability and network noise** locally (increase background Poisson or decrease inhibitory synapse strength)
  - **Extracellular ionic disruption**: transiently increase extracellular Ca2+ (or Mg) or adjust synaptic Mg manipulations if supported
  - **Alter short-term plasticity parameters** to reflect increased failure or potentiation depending on injury model
  - Optionally include an inflammatory-like modulation (e.g., scaling U_SE via ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation pattern) to change short-term release dynamics

How these map to platform manipulations (schema-friendly)
- neuron_sets: All / ExcitatoryNeurons / InhibitoryNeurons / IDNeuronSet (to remove specific neurons)
- synaptic_manipulations:
  - DisconnectSynapticManipulation: to model neuron loss or disconnection after TBI
  - ConnectSynapticManipulation / scaling: to reduce synapse counts or weights
  - SynapticMgManipulation or ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation: to alter extracellular ionic environment / short-term plasticity
- recordings block: SomaVoltageRecording and TimeWindowSomaVoltageRecording
- initialize: Circuit selection (CA1 microcircuit/circuit-from-ID) and v_init

Suggested comparison metrics (to compute from simulation results)
- Population mean firing rates (exc vs inh)
- Spike-count distributions and CV
- Evoked response amplitude and latency (average across trials)
- Pairwise synchrony and spectral power (if LFP proxy computed)
- Changes in effective connectivity: average in-degree/out-degree, synapse counts after manipulations
- Fraction of neurons failing to respond to stimulus (reliability)

Experimental plan / analysis pipeline
1. Build or select a CA1 circuit (existing circuit on platform or assemble point/biophysical neurons).
2. Baseline validation: run Control Adult simulation to ensure stable spontaneous activity and response to stimulus.
3. Create Aging and TBI variants by applying synaptic and neuron-set manipulations described above.
4. For each condition, run N repeats (different seeds) to sample variability (I recommend N ≥ 10).
5. Collect recordings and compute metrics above; perform statistical comparisons (paired or nonparametric tests as appropriate).
6. Visualize: raster plots, per-population firing rate curves, evoked PSTHs, connectivity/synapse summary tables.

Estimated compute needs
- Small CA1 microcircuit with biophysical neurons: moderate compute per run; 1000 ms per trial is reasonable. If you want larger region-scale (CA1+CA3+DG) or high cell counts, prepare for longer run times and more compute credits.

---

## Example concrete parameter set I will use by default (if you select Option A)
- Aging:
  - Excitatory synapse scaling: 0.75 (i.e., 25% reduction)
  - Inhibitory synaptic weight scaling: 0.9
  - Background Poisson rate: +15%
  - random_seed: 1 (can sweep)
- TBI:
  - Local neuron loss: remove 20% of excitatory neurons in the damaged node_set
  - Local connectivity scale: 0.6 (40% reduction)
  - Background Poisson rate in lesion area: +30%
  - extracellular_calcium_concentration in lesion area: 1.5 mM (transient effect)
  - random_seed: 1

I’ll implement these using combinations of:
- neuron_sets (IDNeuronSet for neuron removal)
- synaptic_manipulations: DisconnectSynapticManipulation, SynapticMgManipulation, ScaleAcetylcholineUSESynapticManipulation, ConnectSynapticManipulation (scaling)

---

## What I will do next if you pick Option A
- I will prepare two CircuitSimulationScanConfig entries in the shared state:
  - One for the **Aging** variant
  - One for the **Adult + TBI** variant  
  Each config will include initialize.circuit (selecting a CA1 circuit or circuit ID you provide), simulation_length, neuron_sets, synaptic_manipulations, stimuli, and recordings.
- I will then validate the shared state (run validatestate) so the configuration is ready to generate simulations in the UI.

If you want me to proceed and create the configs now, reply:
- “Proceed with defaults (Option A)” — and optionally provide any answers to the clarifying Qs above (scale, age values, TBI type, simulation_length, seeds, circuit ID if you already have one).
- Or choose “Option B” to receive the full detailed plan without me modifying the shared state.

If you already have a specific circuit ID on the platform or dataset you want used, paste it and I’ll use it in the configuration.

platform_overview_capabilities.user: Explain what is the OpenBrain Institute virtual lab and what can I do in it exactly? Can I model a spiking neural network and run AI models (like reinforcement learning) on them in this platform?
platform_overview_capabilities.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation about the \virtual lab\ feature. I'll search the docs now.## Summary — What the Virtual Lab is
- **Virtual Lab** is the platform’s primary collaborative workspace.  
- It lets you **create and manage projects**, **invite and manage members** with role-based access (Owner / Admin / Member), and **allocate & monitor credits** (the computational budget used for simulations and model-building).  
- Each Virtual Lab is an independent environment so you can separate teams, projects, or workflows.

Key Virtual Lab capabilities (from the docs)
- Create and manage multiple projects inside a lab
- Invite and manage members and assign roles
- Allocate credits to projects and workflows
- Monitor credit usage and view estimates before execution

(Documentation source: Virtual Lab product page — “Virtual Lab”.)

---

## What you can do in the platform (relevant to modeling & simulation)
The platform provides an integrated modeling and simulation stack (OBI-one) that supports biophysically detailed and circuit-level workflows. Relevant features documented include:

- Small circuit simulations (OBI-one)
  - **Supported scale:** “Small circuits” of up to 20 biophysical neurons (3–20 neurons are optimized as small circuits).
  - **Circuit format:** SONATA circuit format is used to describe circuits (nodes, edges, node sets).
  - **Simulation backend:** BlueCelluLab / NEURON (MPI support for distributed runs).
  - **Configuration options:** Simulation length, timestep, v_init, extracellular calcium, random seed, spike detection location, etc.
  - **Neuron sets:** Flexible node_set definitions (AllNeurons, IDNeuronSet, PropertyNeuronSet, combinations).
  - **Synaptic mechanisms:** Built-in synaptic mechanisms (e.g., ProbAMPANMDA_EMS, ProbGABAAB_EMS) with probabilistic release and single-vesicle minis.
  - **Stimuli:** Current clamps, spike-based (Poisson) inputs, targeted stimuli to neuron sets.
  - **Recordings / outputs:** Soma voltage traces, spike times, HDF5 spike files, NWB outputs, and other reports.
  - **Synaptic manipulations:** Connection overrides and manipulations (e.g., magnesium block, neuromodulatory scaling).
  - **Generation workflow:** OBI-one generates SONATA simulation_config.json and node_sets.json; staging of circuit files; executes via the same NEURON-based execution layer used elsewhere.
  - **Result collection:** Gathers voltage traces and spikes across MPI ranks.

(Documentation source: OBI-one circuit-simulation / “Small Circuit Simulations” doc.)

- Paired neuron and single-cell simulation workflows are also supported (for 1–2 neuron experiments and detailed ME-model simulations). (See paired-neuron and single-cell docs.)

- Platform constraints noted in other docs / FAQ:
  - Plasticity-specific datasets or plasticity logic are currently not implemented (FAQ: “no plasticity logic implemented”).
  - Some planned features (e.g., Functional Networks module) do not currently exist (FAQ).

---

## Can you model a spiking neural network on this platform?
Yes — within the documented capabilities:
- You can configure and run **biophysically detailed spiking networks** as SONATA circuits and simulate small circuits (up to 20 biophysical neurons) with synapses, stimuli, and full recording support.
- You can run paired-neuron and single-cell simulations as well.

brain_structures_movement.user: What are the structures of the brain mainly involved in movement?
brain_structures_movement.ai_response:
## Overview — structures mainly involved in movement

Movement is produced by distributed circuits spanning cortex, subcortex, brainstem and spinal cord. Below are the principal structures and their core roles.

### 1) Motor cortex (cerebral cortex)
- **Primary motor cortex (M1; Brodmann area 4)**  
  - Directly issues commands for voluntary, finely controlled movements. Major source of the corticospinal tract.
- **Premotor cortex (lateral premotor area)**  
  - Plans movements in response to external cues; involved in selecting actions and coordinating proximal/axial muscles.
- **Supplementary motor area (SMA; medial frontal cortex)**  
  - Involved in internally generated sequences, bimanual coordination and movement planning.
- **Posterior parietal cortex** (sensorimotor integration)  
  - Integrates sensory information (visual, somatosensory) for planning and guiding actions.

### 2) Basal ganglia (subcortical nuclei)
- **Striatum (caudate + putamen)** — input stage; receives cortical signals  
- **Globus pallidus (internal/external segments, GPi/GPe)** — output and relay nodes  
- **Subthalamic nucleus (STN)** — modulates output through excitatory drive  
- **Substantia nigra (pars compacta (SNc) and pars reticulata (SNr))** — dopaminergic modulation (SNc) and output (SNr)  
- Function: selection and initiation of voluntary movements, suppression of unwanted movements, reinforcement/learning of action sequences through cortico–basal ganglia–thalamic loops.

### 3) Cerebellum
- Key for coordination, timing, motor learning, error correction and adjusting ongoing movements.  
- Receives sensory and motor plan information and returns corrective signals via deep cerebellar nuclei to motor thalamus and brainstem.

### 4) Thalamus
- Motor thalamic nuclei (e.g., ventral lateral, ventral anterior) relay processed signals from basal ganglia and cerebellum back to motor cortical areas — crucial in motor loop communication.

### 5) Brainstem motor centers
- **Red nucleus** — contributes to limb control (rubrospinal tract in some species; modulatory role in humans).  
- **Reticular formation (pontine and medullary reticular formation)** — posture, locomotion, muscle tone via reticulospinal tracts.  
- **Vestibular nuclei** — balance and head/eye reflexes; vestibulospinal tracts maintain posture and extensor tone.  
- These nuclei mediate many automatic and postural components of movement and provide descending drive to spinal circuits.

### 6) Descending motor pathways (tracts)
- **Corticospinal tract (pyramidal tract)** — major pathway for voluntary, skilled distal movements; directly (and indirectly) influences spinal motor neurons.  
- **Corticobulbar tract** — controls cranial nerve motor nuclei (face, jaw, tongue, swallowing).  
- **Reticulospinal, vestibulospinal, rubrospinal tracts** — mediate posture, locomotion, and gross movements; important for involuntary adjustments and synergy patterns.

### 7) Spinal cord circuits
- **Lower (alpha) motor neurons** — final common pathway to skeletal muscle (neuromuscular junction).  
- **Interneurons and central pattern generators (CPGs)** — coordinate reflexes and rhythmic movements (e.g., walking) and integrate descending and sensory inputs.

### 8) Sensory systems (supporting role)
- **Somatosensory system** (proprioception, touch) and **visual/vestibular systems** provide critical feedback for planning, error correction and adaptation of movement.

## How these parts interact (brief)
- Motor planning begins in premotor/SMA and parietal cortex → signals routed to M1 for execution.  
- Basal ganglia and cerebellum process cortical plans in parallel (basal ganglia: selection/initiation; cerebellum: timing/coordination/error correction) and send modulatory outputs to motor thalamus → cortex.  
- Cortex and brainstem produce descending commands via corticospinal and brainstem tracts → spinal CPGs/interneurons → lower motor neurons → muscles.  
- Sensory feedback constantly updates this loop for online correction and motor learning.

## Clinical relevance (short)
- Lesions: M1 or corticospinal damage → weakness/paralysis and loss of fine control.  
- Basal ganglia disorders (e.g., Parkinson’s, Huntington’s) → bradykinesia, tremor, dyskinesias, impaired initiation.  
- Cerebellar lesions → ataxia, dysmetria, impaired coordination.  
- Spinal cord injury → disrupted voluntary movement below lesion.

entitysdk_library.ai_response:
I'll look up the documentation for the entitysdk package (resolving its library ID first).## Summary — What is the entitysdk library?

**entitysdk** is the official Python Software Development Kit for interacting with the Open Brain Platform’s **entitycore** service.  
It provides a type-safe, Pydantic-based client that simplifies authentication, project context management, and CRUD operations on neuroscience entities (cell morphologies, circuits, simulations, ion channel models, assets, etc.). The SDK also includes helpers for uploading/downloading files (single files, directories, multipart streaming) and convenient entity models for programmatic use inside analysis notebooks.

---

## Main features

- **Client-centric API**: single `Client` class that manages API URL, project context, and authentication tokens.
- **Typed entity models**: Pydantic models for entities (e.g., `CellMorphology`, `Circuit`, `Simulation`) for safe request/response handling.
- **Project context handling**: supports working within a project/virtual lab context (ProjectContext).
- **Authentication helpers**: integrates with obi-auth for getting tokens, and accepts token managers.
- **File/asset management**: upload/download single files, directories, multipart uploads, and streaming.
- **Notebook-friendly examples**: example notebooks demonstrating initialization, entity retrieval, and asset handling.

---

## Typical usage in a Python notebook

Below are concise, practical examples (sourced from the entitysdk docs and example notebooks) showing initialization, authentication, retrieving an entity, and downloading an asset.

### 1) Initialization / Client setup

```python
import os
from entitysdk.client import Client
from entitysdk.common import ProjectContext

# API endpoint (example)
entitycore_api_url = \http://127.0.0.1:8000"

# Project context (example UUIDs)
project_context = ProjectContext(
    virtual_lab_id=\a98b7abc-fc46-4700-9e3d-37137812c730",
    project_id=\0dbced5f-cc3d-488a-8c7f-cfb8ea039dc6",
)

# Token (from environment or token manager)
token = os.getenv(\ACCESS_TOKEN", \XXX")

# Create client
client = Client(api_url=entitycore_api_url, project_context=project_context, token_manager=token)
```

### 2) Authenticate (example using obi-auth)

```python
from obi_auth import get_token

# Retrieve a token for the target environment (e.g., \staging")
token = get_token(environment=\staging")

# Pass token into Client (or use token manager)
client = Client(api_url=entitycore_api_url, project_context=project_context, token_manager=token)
```

### 3) Retrieve a single entity by ID

```python
from uuid import UUID
from entitysdk import models

# Fetch a morphology by UUID
morphology = client.get_entity(
    entity_id=UUID(\12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012"),
    entity_type=models.CellMorphology
)

# Fetch a circuit (optionally admin=True for privileged access)
circuit = client.get_entity(
    entity_id=UUID(\87654321-4321-4321-4321-210987654321"),
    entity_type=models.Circuit,
    admin=True
)
```

Returned objects are Pydantic models with typed fields you can inspect and use directly in the notebook.

### 4) Download an asset (file) attached to an entity

The SDK provides helpers to download files or nested asset structures. Example pattern:

```python
# Assume `morphology` is an entity object returned above and has assets metadata
assets = morphology.assets  # inspect available asset records (names, ids, urls)

# Download a specific asset by asset id (pseudo-API; actual method names follow entitysdk client)
asset_id = assets[0].id
local_path = client.download_asset(asset_id=asset_id, target_path=\downloads/")

print(f\Downloaded to {local_path}")
```

Notes:
- The SDK supports streaming and multipart downloads for large files and directory downloads.
- Exact helper method names may vary (see client methods in the SDK docs/examples); use the client’s download helpers shown in the example notebooks.

---

## Where to find examples and docs

- The SDK repository contains example notebooks demonstrating initialization and common workflows (e.g., `examples/06_analysis_notebook.ipynb`).
- The README describes usage patterns (auth, client, file upload/download, entity models).

---

If you want, I can:
- Show the exact client method names for downloading assets by looking up the client API in the docs, or
- Open the notebook example and extract the concrete download helper call used there.

Which would you prefer?

The Open Brain Platform (OBI) is a cloud-integrated environment for building, exploring, and running reproducible, provenance-tracked neuroscience models and analyses. It combines an atlas-driven data browser, standardized modeling workflows (the **OBI-one** library), and tools for registering, sharing and simulating data-driven neural models — from ion-channel fitting and neuron skeletonization to paired-neuron and circuit (microcircuit) simulations.

Key capabilities described in the documentation:
- Discover experimental data, computational models, simulations, and literature via the **Explore** interface.
- Run standardized modeling workflows (OBI-one) for tasks such as:
  - Ion channel model building and registration
  - Neuron skeletonization / morphological analysis
  - Paired-neuron simulations
  - Circuit / microcircuit simulations (small circuits and larger circuit campaigns)
- Preserve provenance for workflows, support parameter scans, and provide programmatic APIs (OBI-one) for reproducible pipelines.

(References: Documentation pages: “Explore Data Guide”, “Build Ion Channel Model”, “Neuron Skeletonization — Overview”, “Paired Neuron Simulation — Overview”, “Circuit Simulation — Overview”.)

## Who is it designed for?

The platform is explicitly aimed at neuroscience researchers and teams who need standardized, reproducible computational workflows and integrated data access. Typical user groups (inferred from the documented product-focused workflows and features):

- Experimental neuroscientists who want to register and reuse electrophysiology / morphology data and link raw data to downstream models (e.g., ion channel fits, neuron skeletons).

- Data discovery and reproducible analysis: the Explore interface supports locating experimental data, models, and literature, enabling integrated analysis pipelines that link data discovery to modeling workflows and simulations.

## Platform features that enable these applications

I'll check the platform documentation about circuit simulation and building models. I'll look up relevant docs now.## Short answer — is it possible here?
Yes — you can build a digital fly brain on the Open Brain Platform, but there are important platform constraints and practical steps to follow. The platform’s simulation and circuit tools are designed around SONATA-style circuits and the OBI “circuit” workflows (design → generate → execute). To use fly connectomes you will typically need to: (1) convert the connectome into the platform’s circuit data model (SONATA nodes/edges + metadata), (2) upload/register the circuit (or import via the API), (3) use the platform’s circuit analysis and simulation tooling (obi-one / obione endpoints and the Virtual Lab) to inspect, validate and run simulations.

Below I give a clear, step-by-step workflow, required formats, recommended tools on the platform, practical tips, limitations you should expect, and options I can help with next.

---

## Recommended end-to-end workflow

### 1) Clarify scope and scale
- Decide which scale you want to build first:
  - Single neuropil / small circuit (hundreds–thousands of neurons)
  - Brain region or whole fly brain (millions of synapses; heavy compute)
- Start small: prototype with a neuropil (e.g., antennal lobe, optic lobe module) before scaling to the entire brain.

### 2) Gather & preprocess the connectome data
- Collect the connectome dataset(s) you plan to use (FlyEM hemibrain, FAFB, other EM reconstructions, or published graph exports).
- Preprocess steps typically include:
  - Node table: assign unique neuron IDs, anatomical region, cell types or m-types, 3D soma coordinates, morphological metadata, excitatory/inhibitory type, and model template mapping
  - Edge table: pre/post node ids, synapse counts/weights, synapse positions (x,y,z), synapse types (chemical/electrical), synapse delays if available
  - Clean duplicates, reconcile IDs across sources, and annotate missing fields you will need for simulation (e.g., excitatory/inhibitory)

### 3) Map connectome fields to SONATA / platform circuit model
- The platform expects SONATA-style data for circuit simulations:
  - Nodes file(s): population name(s), node_id, node_type_id, x,y,z, and property columns (e.g., mtype, layer, region, excitatory)
  - Edges file(s): source node id, target node id, synapse properties (weight, delay), synapse positions
- Map your fly-specific metadata (cell types, neuropil labels) onto the platform’s property schema. Create a node population naming scheme (e.g., Fly_AL_PNs, Fly_AL_LNs).
- If you don’t have biophysical cell models for fly neurons, you can:
  - Use simplified point-neuron models (leaky integrate-and-fire, GLIF) supported by the platform
  - Use hybrid approaches: morphologies + simplified electrical templates (ME-models) where available

### 4) Convert to supported file formats
- Export nodes/edges as SONATA-compatible HDF5/CSV files (SONATA nodes/edges + node_types, edge_types).
- Create a small manifest / metadata JSON describing populations and model templates.
- Ensure proper units and coordinate systems; annotate species and provenance in metadata.

### 5) Upload & register on the platform (Virtual Lab)
- Use your Virtual Lab to organize the project and allocate credits. The platform’s Virtual Lab is the workspace for model building and running simulations.
- Import the SONATA files into the platform. Typical options:
  - Upload files to the project storage and create a new circuit entity (via the UI or API)
  - If the platform provides an import wizard for SONATA, follow the steps to declare node populations and edge populations
- If a direct import for Drosophila is not available, register the circuit as a custom \microcircuit\ or egion\ with appropriate metadata.

### 6) Inspect & validate with obione tools
- Use the platform’s circuit-metrics endpoint to inspect your circuit structure:
  - **obione-circuitmetrics-getone** (level_of_detail_nodes=1) — returns node populations, property names and unique values, node sets, edge populations and basic statistics. This is essential to:
    - Verify property columns were ingested correctly
    - See available node sets (e.g., “Excitatory”, “Inhibitory”) and property values you can use for filtering
- Use **obione-circuitconnectivitymetrics-getone** to compute:
  - Connection probabilities between groups
  - Mean synapses per connection
  - Group results by mtype, layer, synapse_class, etc.
- Use **circuit-population-data-analysis** to run natural-language queries about your node populations (e.g., counts per neuropil, soma coordinate distributions).

(I can call those exact tools for you once your circuit is registered — I’ll need the circuit_id and, for connectivity metrics, the edge_population names returned by obione-circuitmetrics-getone.)

### 7) Choose neuron models and simulation parameters
- For each node_type / mtype decide:
  - Biophysical model (multi-compartment) — requires morphology + e-model templates
  - Morpho-electrical (ME) models — morphology + electrical parameters
  - Point neuron (e.g., LIF, HH reduced) — smaller compute footprint
- Assign synapse models (conductance-based, exponential synapses) and parameters (gmax, tau, delay). If the connectome provides synapse counts, use them to scale weights.

### 8) Build a SimulationCampaign and generate runs
- Use the platform’s simulation workflow:
  - Design a SimulationCampaign (parameter sweeps, durations, stimuli)
  - Generate Simulations (frontend will expand the campaign into concrete Simulations)
- Validate the configuration (the platform supports state management through chat — I can help fill the configuration fields).

### 9) Execute simulations (credits & compute)
- Estimate credit usage: complexity depends on number of neurons, synapses, simulation duration, and chosen solver.
- Start with short test runs and small populations. Scale up after verification.
- Inspect SimulationResult outputs (SONATA files, spike trains, traces) and iterate.

### 10) Analysis & visualization
- Use the platform’s visualization tools to view morphologies and traces.
- Use connectivity metrics again on simulation-subsets to compare structure vs function.

---

## Important platform constraints & practical caveats

- Species support: The platform’s core atlas-driven data (pre-built atlases, region datasets) focuses on mouse/rat/human. Building a fly brain is still possible as a user-supplied/custom circuit, but you will need to supply your own species-specific metadata and region definitions. Expect to treat your fly connectome as a custom microcircuit/region when importing.
- Data size: Full fly connectomes (e.g., whole-brain EM graphs) can be very large (many millions of synapses). This will be heavy to import, inspect, and simulate — plan for staged import (by neuropil) and significant compute/credit usage.
- Model availability: The platform has many biophysical and point neuron templates for vertebrate neurons. Fly-specific biophysical templates are rarer; you will likely need to:
  - Create or adapt point-neuron or simplified models for fly neurons
  - Or provide your own e-models if you have them
- Provenance and mapping: Fly connectome cell type labels vary across datasets (hemibrain vs FAFB). Carefully record mappings and provenance in metadata to avoid misinterpretation.
- Tooling: The platform’s analysis tools (obione-circuitmetrics, obione-circuitconnectivitymetrics, circuit-population-data-analysis) expect properly defined node/edge properties (strings for property values). Use obione-circuitmetrics-getone with level_of_detail_nodes=1 to discover what properties were recognized.

---

## Concrete list of platform tools to use (in order)
1. Use Virtual Lab to create a project and allocate credits.
2. Upload SONATA nodes/edges and metadata files to your project storage.
3. Register/import the circuit (entitycore-circuit-getall / entitycore-circuit-getone can be used to list and query circuits once created).
4. Call obione-circuitmetrics-getone with your circuit_id, level_of_detail_nodes=1 to inspect node/edge populations and property values.
5. Use obione-circuitconnectivitymetrics-getone to compute connection probabilities and synapse statistics for chosen edge_populations and pre/post filters.
6. Use circuit-population-data-analysis for ad-hoc ENQL-style population queries (natural language).
7. Design SimulationCampaign via the state-management workflow (I can help fill fields).
8. Generate and Execute simulations; then retrieve SimulationResults and analyze.

- Start with a small SONATA example (10–100 neurons) to validate metadata ingestion and tooling.

- For edges, include synapse_count and a normalized weight, and a synapse_class label for grouping.
- Keep property values as strings (platform connectivity tools require string property values).
- Version your imports and include a README with dataset provenance and conversion notes.

---

## Next steps — how I can help right now

  - Draft a minimal SONATA nodes/edges template for Fly data you have (example CSV/HDF5 headers and a small toy dataset).
  - Help convert a section of your connectome (if you paste a sample nodes/edges excerpt) into SONATA-compatible files.
  - Once you register a circuit on the platform, I can call:

    - **obione-circuitconnectivitymetrics-getone** to compute connectivity statistics you care about.
  - Help design a SimulationCampaign and populate the UI state (the platform’s state-management supports that).

  - B) You upload or paste a small sample nodes/edges table and I’ll convert it into SONATA-ready CSVs and show how to register it.

calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.expected_output: Select a hippocampal pyramidal ME-model. At present, the public rat and mouse hippocampus ME-Models have the brain region `Field CA1`, e-type `cACpyr` (for rat models and `cADpyr` for mouse models) and m-type `SP_PC`. Select one such pyramidal neuron model. I'll broaden the search to 'tufted' and 'layer 5' and also check emodel mtype labels like 'L5 pyramidal cell' variants.## Overview

Below I outline (A) what to look for in an ME-model to produce calcium-mediated plateau potentials in the apical dendrite, (B) recommended ion-channel / calcium mechanism parameter ranges and compartment placement, and (C) a ready-to-run simulation configuration (protocol, recording, numerical settings) you can use as a starting point. If you want, I can next search the ME-model catalog again for specific candidate ME-models (mouse or rat L5/L2/3 pyramidal ME-models) — tell me which species/layer you prefer and I'll check.

---

## A — Required ME-model features (choose a model that has these)

- **Apical dendritic morphology with an extended tuft** (distal apical compartments). Plateaus require spatially distributed dendritic compartments.
- **Active Ca2+ channels in apical dendrite** (both low-voltage T-type and/or high-voltage L-type/P/Q-type may contribute). The model should expose per-compartment channel density properties so you can inspect/adjust them.
- **Calcium concentration dynamics** (internal [Ca2+] variable), including:
  - Calcium influx through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (and NMDA receptors if present).
  - Calcium buffering and extrusion mechanisms (pump or exponential decay).
- **NMDA synapses or synaptic conductances** available for adding clustered synaptic input to tuft (NMDA can potentiate/maintain plateaus).
- **Per-compartment mechanism control** (so you can raise Ca channel density in distal apical branches if needed).

If an ME-model lacks these, you can often add/modify channels and calcium handling in the emulator (NEURON/obi-one) but choosing an ME-model that already contains them is faster.

---

## B — Ion channel & calcium mechanism recommendations

Use these as target densities and kinetics to promote dendritic calcium spikes and sustained plateaus. Exact values depend on the model's units and channel implementations — treat ranges as a starting point.

- Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels
  - **High-voltage-activated (CaL/CaN)** (distal apical tuft)
    - Conductance density: ~0.1 – 1.0 mS/cm^2 in distal apical branches (start low and increase)
  - **Low-voltage-activated (CaT)** (proximal apical/proximal tuft)
    - Conductance density: ~0.01 – 0.2 mS/cm^2
- Sodium and potassium channels (dendritic)
  - Sodium (Na): maintain enough density to support local regenerative depolarization; tuft Na ~10–50% of somatic Na.
  - A-type K (KA): moderate density can shape spikes; reduce KA in tuft if you want easier plateau generation.
- Calcium reversal potential (E_Ca)
  - Typical: +120 mV (depends on implementation)
- Calcium dynamics (internal [Ca2+])
  - **Decay (pump) tau**: 20–200 ms (short decay -> less sustained plateaus; longer decay supports longer plateau)
    - Start with tau_Ca ≈ 80–150 ms in tuft for plateaus
  - **Resting [Ca2+]**: ~50–100 nM (set by model)
  - **Buffering**: include mobile buffer if available; moderate buffering slows accumulation (reduces plateau), low buffering makes plateaus easier
  - **Pump strength** (or rate constant): tune to match desired decay tau
- NMDA receptors (optional but effective)
  - NMDA conductances clustered on tuft can maintain depolarized plateaus via the voltage-dependence + Mg2+ unblock
  - g_NMDA per synapse: start ~0.5–2 nS for clustered synchronous inputs (depends on synapse implementation)
  - Include realistic NMDA time constants (rise ~5 ms, decay ~50–150 ms)

Notes:
- Plateaus often emerge when Ca influx (VGCC + NMDAR) outcompetes local outward currents and extrusion.
- Many published models achieve dendritic plateau by elevating CaL (L-type) density and reducing KA in distal tuft.

---

## C — Simulation protocol and numerical settings (recommended)

Use these settings to reveal apical-dendrite Ca2+ plateau potentials.

Simulation basics
- Simulator: NEURON (or OBI-one that uses NEURON backend)
- Temperature: 32–34 °C (affects channel kinetics)
- Simulation length: 1.0–2.0 s (plateaus may last 100s of ms to >1 s)
- Integration time step: **dt = 0.02 ms** (0.01–0.05 ms acceptable). If using CVode, ensure accuracy tolerances are tight (rtol ~1e-6).
- Spatial discretization: ensure sections have nseg set so compartment length ≤ 20 µm (or lambda rule) for correct dendritic dynamics.

Stimulation protocol to elicit a dendritic plateau
- Protocol 1 — Distal tuft current injection:
  - Inject long (200–500 ms) depolarizing current pulse into a distal apical tuft compartment (or group of tuft compartments).
  - Current amplitude: start with 0.2–1.0 nA (adjust until dendritic regenerative event occurs); if unit differences exist, use conductance-based synapses instead.
  - Ramp or sustained: a brief ramp (20–50 ms) into a sustained pulse helps recruit Ca channels without over-shocking the dendrite.
- Protocol 2 — Clustered excitatory synaptic barrage:
  - Place 20–100 excitatory synapses (AMPA+NMDA) clustered on distal tuft branches.
  - Activate them synchronously or in a high-frequency burst (e.g., 50–200 Hz bursts, 5–20 inputs per burst) for 50–200 ms. NMDA-mediated depolarization + Ca influx favors plateau maintenance.
- Combine somatic current injection with tuft stimulation if you want backpropagation-assisted plateau initiation.

Recording
- Record membrane potential at:
  - Soma
  - Proximal apical trunk
  - Distal apical tuft (where stimulation is applied)
- Record intracellular calcium concentration ([Ca2+]) in the same distal tuft compartment(s).
- Optionally record channel currents for Ca (I_Ca), Na (I_Na), K (I_K), and NMDA currents.

Expected signature of calcium-mediated plateau
- Rapid dendritic depolarization to a plateau (tens of mV above resting), sustained for 100s of ms.
- Large local increase in [Ca2+] coincident with the plateau.
- Somatic response: sustained depolarization and possibly high-frequency spike output riding on the plateau (or no somatic spikes if plateau remains localized).

Tuning tips
- If plateau fails to appear:
  - Increase distal CaL (or CaT) density incrementally.
  - Reduce A-type K density in tuft.
  - Increase calcium decay tau (weaker pump) to allow higher [Ca2+] accumulation.
  - Add or increase NMDA conductances and cluster synapses.
  - Increase number or synchrony of tuft synaptic inputs.
- If plateau is too easy / too long:
  - Decrease Ca channel densities or increase pump strength (reduce tau).
  - Increase KA density.
- Monitor [Ca2+] to ensure unrealistic saturation — if so, strengthen pump or buffering.

Numerical/solver caution
- Ca dynamics and slow plateaus can be stiff. If using fixed dt, use small dt. CVode (variable time-step) helps but be careful with fast channel kinetics; test accuracy with dt reductions.
- If adding many synapses, watch performance — start with a single tuft branch cluster for testing.

---

## D — Example configuration (pseudo-NEURON / parameter summary)

- Temperature: 33 C
- dt = 0.02 ms; tstop = 1500 ms
- Stimulus:
  - Location: distal apical tuft segment (choose section ~600–900 µm from soma)
  - Type: current clamp (IClamp) or clustered synaptic input
  - For IClamp: delay = 100 ms; dur = 300 ms; amp = 0.4 nA (tune)
- Channels (distal apical branches):
  - g_CaL = 0.3 mS/cm2 (increase up to 1.0)
  - g_CaT = 0.05 mS/cm2
  - g_Na = 0.2 × somatic g_Na
  - g_KA = 0.5 × default (reduce to ease plateau)
- Calcium mechanism:
  - cai_decay_tau = 100 ms (or pump rate adjusted to match)
  - depth (shell) = 0.1 µm (depends on mechanism)
- Synapses (alternate approach):
  - 50 synapses on clustered tuft segments
  - AMPA g_peak = 0.5 nS, tau_decay = 2 ms
  - NMDA g_peak = 1.0 nS, tau_decay = 80–150 ms

---

## E — Do you want me to find a ready ME-model on the platform?

I attempted an initial catalog search for mouse pyramidal/Layer-5 ME-models but did not find direct matches with the search strings I tried. If you want, tell me:
- The species (mouse, rat, human)
- Layer preference (L5, L2/3, L6)
- Any preferred model source (e.g., a published L5 pyramidal model)

I will then search the ME-model catalog for ME-models that:
- Contain apical tuft morphology
- Include Ca2+ dynamics and dendritic Ca channels
- Are available as ME-models ready to simulate

If you prefer, I can also produce a NEURON (.hoc/.py) snippet that implements the simulation protocol above for a canonical L5 pyramidal morphology (you'd supply or I can try to find a morphology/model to attach).

- B) Generate a NEURON simulation script example using the parameter set above, or

m_vs_e_model_explanation.user: Explain the difference between m-model and e-model.
m_vs_e_model_explanation.expected_output:
The m-model is a detailed 3D reconstructed or synthesised morphology. A 3D reconstructed morphology is generated by tracing a microscopic images of biological neurons. The images are usally obtained using usally thin slices of tissue plated on a glass. Some reconstructed morphologies are obtained by `skeletonisation` of electron microscopic meshses (e.g. MiCRONS morphology meshes). The reconstructed morphologies usually undergo a pipeline to correct and fix for problems which can cause issues during simualtions. Synthesised morphologies are generated by topological synthesis using features from multiple morphologies. 
An e-model or electrical model is a usually a NEURON simulator-based single neuron model which is constructed by adding ion channel models to an reconstructed or synthesised morphology to generate electrical activity. The e-models are optimised using multi-objective optimisation (e.g. using BluePyEModel or BluePyOpt) to generate specific firing patterns as seen in real electrophysiological recordings. These models are also validated by using protocols and electrical features or e-features (e.g. using eFEL python library) which were not used for optimisation. E-models form a template for build more such neuron models by combining with morphologies (m-model) to generate me-models.  The main differences between an e-model and me-model is that e-models are optimised and non-simulatable (on the platform). Whereas me-models are formed by combining m-model and e-model, and can be simulated on the platform. The me-models are not optimised for any cobination of m-model and e-model.

- **E-model** — defined in the platform glossary as an *electrical model* (fits ion-channel / membrane parameters to electrophysiology).  
- **M-model** — there is **no** glossary entry for the term “m-model”. Do you mean **Morphology** (the structural neuron description) or **ME-model** (morphoelectric model = morphology + e-model)? I list the relevant platform definitions below.

## Platform glossary definitions (from documentation)
### **E-model**
- **Definition:** An E-model, or *electrical model*, is a computational model that represents the electrical properties of a neuron.  
- **Key points:**  
  - Generated by fitting model parameters to experimental electrophysiological data (membrane potential traces).  
  - Typically obtained with multi-objective evolutionary algorithms.  
  - Used to simulate and analyse neuronal excitability and responses to stimuli.  
  - Built/analysed with tools such as BluePyEModel, BluePyOpt and BlueCelluLab.

### **Morphology**
- **Definition:** A neuronal *morphology* describes the structural shape of a neuron (soma, dendrites — apical and basal, axon).  
- **Key points:**  
  - Stored in file formats like .h5, .swc, or ASCII.  
  - Used to identify cell types and build computational neuron/circuit models.  
  - Analysed with NeuroM and TMD.

### **ME-model**
- **Definition:** An **ME-model** (morphoelectric model) is the combination of an E-model with the relevant morphology.  
- **Key points:**  
  - Combines electrical properties and morphological structure so the model is ready for biophysically detailed simulation (e.g., in BlueCelluLab).  
  - Serves as an essential element for building circuits on the platform.

## Recommended follow-up
- If by “m-model” you meant **morphology alone**, then the difference is:
  - Morphology = structural description only.
  - E-model = electrical parameters only.
  - ME-model = both combined (morphology + e-model).
- If you meant something else by “m-model”, tell me which term you intended and I’ll fetch the exact platform definition or examples.

Would you like links to the glossary entries or examples of e-model / ME-model entities on the platform?

how_does_it_work.user: How does the platform work? Can you explain how it operates?
how_does_it_work.expected_output: The platform is organized around Virtual Labs containing Projects, where users can explore neuroscience data via a 3D brain atlas (Explore section), build computational neuron models (Workflows > Build), run simulations at various scales from single neurons to microcircuits (Workflows > Simulate), and analyze results in Jupyter notebooks. Data flows from experimental measurements through model building to simulation, with an integrated AI assistant and literature search to support the research workflow.
how_does_it_work.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation to give an accurate overview. I'll call the platform docs tool now.## Overview — How the Open Brain Platform operates

Below is a concise, structured summary of how the Open Brain Platform (OBP) works, based on the platform documentation.

---

## 1) Purpose and scope
- The platform is an atlas-driven system for discovering, standardizing, and simulating neuroscience data across scales (molecular → single neuron → circuits → brain regions).
- It supports experimental data, computational models, and simulation workflows and stores assets with standardized provenance.

---

## 2) Main capabilities and data types
- Data types available:
  - Neuron morphology (axons, soma, dendrites)
  - Electrophysiological recordings
  - Ion channel models
  - Neuron/bouton density, synapses and connections
  - Digital models (E-models, ME-models)
  - Synaptome (connection architecture)
- Digital models and workflows include single-neuron, paired-neuron, small-circuit simulations, and higher-level composed models.

---

## 3) Core components / services
- Explore (Data discovery)
  - Primary UI for browsing brain regions, models, recordings, literature, and simulation artifacts.
  - Workspace modes: Public vs Project, species selection, spatial/brain-region navigation, filtering and downloading records.
  - (Doc: “Explore Data Guide”)
- OBI-one (workflow library)
  - Standardized, reproducible workflows for modeling tasks (ion channel building, neuron skeletonization, paired-neuron simulations, circuit simulations, data contribution).
  - Provides:
    - Provenance tracking
    - Parameter-scan support
    - FastAPI service for programmatic usage and example notebooks
  - (Docs: “Build Ion Channel Model”, “Neuron Skeletonization”, “Paired Neuron Simulation”, “Circuit Simulation”, “Contribute and Fix Data”)
- Entity database and EntityCore (standardized cloud DB)
  - Stores entities (morphologies, ion-channel models, simulation campaigns/results, etc.) with metadata and access controls.
  - Integration with OBI-one via entitysdk.

---

## 4) Simulation lifecycle (high-level)
- Design: Create simulation specification (JSON-schema based for circuits). Define parameters, neuron sets, synapses, manipulations.
- Generation: A SimulationCampaign is created and expanded into individual Simulation configurations (parameter combinations).
- Execution: Simulations run and produce SimulationResult outputs (SONATA format) and execution provenance records.
- Provenance follows a W3C PROV-DM pattern linking campaigns → simulations → results and recording activities (generation/execution).
- (Doc: “Circuit simulations” + simulation model descriptions)

---

## 5) Typical workflows available
- Ion channel model building: Fit channel equations from recordings, produce .mod mechanisms, register outputs as IonChannelModel entities.
- Neuron skeletonization: Process morphologies, extract skeletons, compute morphological features, prepare for simulation.
- Paired neuron simulation: Configure and simulate two-neuron synaptic interactions with parameter sweeps.
- Circuit simulation: Build small microcircuits, define neuron sets and synapses, run parameter scans and analyze emergent dynamics.
- Data contribution & QA: Upload experimental data, validate formats/metadata, fix data quality issues with provenance.

---

## 6) Programmatic & UI access
- Web UI (Explore) for interactive browsing, filtering, download, and launching tasks.
- OBI-one library and FastAPI for programmatic workflows and reproducible pipeline execution (notebooks + examples available).
- Entity SDK (entitysdk) for database integration and managing entities.

---

## 7) Provenance, reproducibility, and best practices
- Workflows and simulations are tracked with standardized provenance (SimulationCampaign → Simulation → SimulationResult).
- Parameter scans and reproducible API-driven workflows are encouraged via OBI-one.
- Documentation and example notebooks are provided in OBI-one for reproducible use.

---

## 8) Where to find the docs referenced here
(Selected documentation pages found in the platform docs)
- Explore Data Guide — Explore section and data discovery
- Circuit simulation — overview and how to run circuit campaigns
- Build Ion Channel Model — workflow details for ion channel fitting
- Neuron Skeletonization — morphology processing workflows
- Paired Neuron Simulation — paired neuron workflows
- Contribute and Fix Data — data contribution and quality-control workflows

---
